Measles Across the Globe

Author

Natalie Cartwright, Zita Tam, Aryan Nair, Amanda Dong

News of measles outbreaks are constantly being released. Despite vaccines, measles continues to affect the whole world. The World Health Organization collects data on monthly measles and rubella cases across the world. We will be focusing on measles cases, as it’s typically more severe and contagious compared to rubella. We were provided with data on suspected measles cases, confirmed cases, and total cases. The World Health Organization provides these counts for each country, and by two time frames: monthly and yearly (2012 to 2025). All data is provisional and is reported by a country’s WHO member each year.

In addition to our measles data, we will also be using data collected from API Ninja on Air Quality for each country. The website does not explicitly say where they collected the Air Quality data from. We were provided with data on several major pollutants, but will focus on the overall Air Quality Index (AQI). The Air Quality Index is a globally used measurement that ranges from 0 to 500, with higher values meaning worse air pollution.The index is calculated based on the 5 major air pollutants. All information on air quality provided are the latest known data.

We are interested in if there is a link to rising measles cases as the years go by and if that could possibly be something to worry about for future decades. Measles was eradicated from the US in 2000 due to high vaccination rates but recently new cases of measles have been reported leading to us wondering what is up with that. Is measles on the rise again and what could have caused its resurgence.

Code 
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(tidyverse)
library(stringr)
library(gt)
library(paletteer)
library(ggridges)

cases_month <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_month.csv')
cases_year <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_year.csv')
meta_df <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/amandaadong/project1/main/metadf.csv")

Measles cases compared over the years

One of the first things we were interested in learning more about is how the number of measles cases has changed over the years. Was there a particular year with a whole bunch of measles cases, is there an overall trend in measles cases or have things stayed the same despite the years changing. To answer this question, we first needed to determine how each country’s total number of measles cases was changing year-by-year. To do this, we compared each sequential year and categorized the change as declining (if the previous year had more cases), surging (if the previous year had less cases), and stable (if cases stayed relatively the same).

Data Summaries

Code 
trend_label <- function(current, previous) {
  pct_change <- (current - previous) / previous * 100
  case_when(
    pct_change < -20 ~ "declining",
    pct_change >  20 ~ "surging",
    .default = "stable"
  )
}

yearly_summary <- cases_year |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    total_cases = sum(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_countries = n_distinct(country),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(year) |>
  mutate(
    prev_total = lag(total_cases),
    pct_change = round((total_cases - prev_total) / prev_total * 100, 1),
    trend = trend_label(total_cases, prev_total)
  ) |>
  select(year, total_cases, n_countries, pct_change, trend)

Table 1: Global Measles Cases by Year (2012–2025)

Code 
yearly_summary |>
  select(year, total_cases, n_countries, pct_change, trend) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    year = md("**Year**"),
    total_cases = md("**Total Cases**"),
    n_countries = md("**Countries Reporting**"),
    pct_change = md("**% Change from Prior Year**"),
    trend = md("**Trend**")
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = total_cases, use_seps = TRUE, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(columns = n_countries, use_seps = TRUE, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(columns = pct_change, decimals = 1, suffix = "%") |>
  data_color(
    columns = pct_change,
    fn = scales::col_numeric(
      palette = c("#D9565C", "#FFFFFF", "#647D4B"),
      domain  = c(-100, 200)
    )
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "#D9565C", weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_body(columns = trend, rows = trend == "declining")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "#647D4B", weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_body(columns = trend, rows = trend == "surging")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "#848C80"),
    locations = cells_body(columns = trend, rows = trend == "stable")
  ) |>
  tab_spanner(
    label = md("**Case Counts**"),
    columns = c(total_cases, n_countries)
  ) |>
  tab_spanner(
    label = md("**Year-over-Year Change**"),
    columns = c(pct_change, trend)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_spanners()
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.width = pct(100),
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    heading.align = "left",
    table.border.top.color = "black",
    row.striping.include_table_body = TRUE
  ) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—")
A table showing the percent change in amount of measles cases from the prior year and if that is stable, surging or declining
Year
Case Counts
Year-over-Year Change
Total Cases Countries Reporting % Change from Prior Year Trend
2012 112,548 150 — stable
2013 178,526 148 58.6 surging
2014 290,888 154 62.9 surging
2015 247,474 176 −14.9 stable
2016 180,015 179 −27.3 declining
2017 168,190 182 −6.6 stable
2018 276,157 182 64.2 surging
2019 541,401 187 96.0 surging
2020 93,840 175 −82.7 declining
2021 59,619 153 −36.5 declining
2022 174,340 168 192.4 surging
2023 321,889 176 84.6 surging
2024 359,534 184 11.7 stable
2025 88,853 168 −75.3 declining

Global measles cases fluctuated during the 2012-2025 period. There was a high peak in 2019 before declining and significantly changing 192.4% in 2022. The decline of measles cases in 2020-2021 could be due to the increase in COVID-19 cases, but more data is needed to understand if COVID-19 was a direct cause.

Figure 1: Percent Change in measles cases

Code 
ggplot(yearly_summary, aes(x = year, xend = year, y = 0, yend = pct_change,
                           color = trend)) +
  geom_segment(size = 1.3, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_point(aes(y = pct_change)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(panel.border = element_blank()) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = yearly_summary$year) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-100, 200, by = 50)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
    labs(title = "Global Measles Cases Over Time", subtitle = "Percent Change Year-to-Year (2012-2025)") +
  xlab("Year") + ylab("Percent Change (%)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(surging = "#647D4BFF",
                      declining = "#D9565CFF",
                      stable = "#848C80"))

Global measles cases from 2012-2025 had a decline or were stable from 2012-2017. Cases dropped significantly in 2020-2021, occurring at the same time COVID-19 was spreading. COVID-19 could have been one of the reasons why measles cases dropped during 2020-2021. Not long after COVID-19, measles cases spiked up in 2022, with nearly a 200% increase. The good news is that in the year of 2025, measles cases have declined by around 75.3% and hopefully will continue on this declining trend.

A large increase in measles cases in 2022 has been offest by a smaller but still large decline in cases in 2025 that hopefully will continue to be the trend in coming years

Methods of Confirming Measles Cases

Not only were we interested in looking at how measles cases changed over time, but we also wanted to know if there has been a change in the methods of finding measles over time. The difference in technology from 2012 to 2025 has drastically changed and we were interested if that change was also present in the technology used to confirm measles cases.The number of total measles cases for each country, month, and year is made up of 3 possible methods of detecting measles: Clinically-compatible, Epidemiologically-linked, and Laboratory-confirmed. Let’s see if the proportion of each method has changed throughout the years.

Code 
methods <- list(clin_prop = "clinical", 
                epi_prop = "epi",
                lab_prop = "lab")

cases_filtered <- cases_year %>% filter(!is.na(measles_clinical),
                                     !is.na(measles_total),
                                     !is.na(measles_epi_linked),
                                     !is.na(measles_lab_confirmed))
yearly_totals <- cases_filtered %>% group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    total = sum(measles_total),
    clinical = sum(measles_clinical),
    epi = sum(measles_epi_linked),
    lab = sum(measles_lab_confirmed),
    .groups = "drop")

props <- function(col) {
  yearly_totals[[col]] / yearly_totals$total
}

propsbyyear <- bind_cols(
  select(yearly_totals, year),
  map_dfc(methods, props)
)

plot_long <- propsbyyear %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(clin_prop, epi_prop, lab_prop),
               names_to = "method",
               values_to = "proportion")

Table 2: Proportion of Measles Cases by Detection Method (2012–2025)

Code 
propsbyyear_styled <- propsbyyear |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    year = md("**Year**"),
    clin_prop = md("**Clinical**"),
    epi_prop = md("**Epi-Linked**"),
    lab_prop = md("**Lab Confirmed**")
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = c(clin_prop, epi_prop, lab_prop), decimals = 1) |>
  tab_spanner(
    label = md("**Proportion of Total Cases by Detection Method**"),
    columns = c(clin_prop, epi_prop, lab_prop)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_spanners()
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.width = pct(100),
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    heading.align = "left",
    table.border.top.color = "black",
    row.striping.include_table_body = TRUE
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "#D4EFDF"),  # light green
    locations = cells_body(
      columns = clin_prop,
      rows = clin_prop == pmax(clin_prop, epi_prop, lab_prop, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "#D4EFDF"),
    locations = cells_body(
      columns = epi_prop,
      rows = epi_prop == pmax(clin_prop, epi_prop, lab_prop, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "#D4EFDF"),
    locations = cells_body(
      columns = lab_prop,
      rows = lab_prop == pmax(clin_prop, epi_prop, lab_prop, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
  )


propsbyyear_styled
A table showing the proportion of total measles cases by their detection method and the highest proportion for each year
Year
Proportion of Total Cases by Detection Method
Clinical Epi-Linked Lab Confirmed
2012 34.9% 23.4% 41.7%
2013 22.4% 34.9% 42.7%
2014 42.5% 22.6% 34.9%
2015 50.9% 20.5% 28.5%
2016 55.4% 18.9% 25.6%
2017 55.3% 18.6% 26.1%
2018 46.3% 21.1% 32.6%
2019 27.2% 51.0% 21.8%
2020 32.6% 28.1% 39.3%
2021 46.6% 11.1% 42.3%
2022 38.5% 31.0% 30.5%
2023 28.3% 27.1% 44.6%
2024 29.5% 23.9% 46.7%
2025 32.1% 18.3% 49.6%

Clinical diagnosis is a consistent method that makes up the majority of the reported cases across 2012-2025, while epi-linked cases is consistent with the smallest share in most years having only one year where they were the highest percentage. Lab confirmed cases did have a gradual increases from around 25-30% in the 2010s period and increased to 40-50% by the 2020s, which suggests there was improvement in lab methods globally. There was a shift from using the lab to using clinical to lab again showing a shift in methods as the years go on.

Figure 2: Detection Method by Year

Code 
ggplot(plot_long, aes(x = year, y = proportion, fill = method)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill", stat = "identity") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = plot_long$year) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8)) +
  labs(title = "Measles Detection Method by Year",
       subtitle = "Clinical vs. Epi-linked vs. Lab Cases") +
  xlab("Year") +
  ylab("Proportion") +
  scale_fill_paletteer_d("nationalparkcolors::Acadia", 
                         labels = c(clin_prop = "Clinical",
                                 epi_prop = "Epi-Linked",
                                 lab_prop = "Lab Confirmed"))

From 2012-2025, the clinical method for measles detection is the most commonly used method. The lab confirmed method tends to have a gradual increase over the years with around 25-30% in 2014-2919 and increasing to 40-50% by 2023-2025. For the epi-linked method, its trend is stable with a huge spike in 2019, with epi-linked cases increasing to 50% that year. Again, in 2020-2021, the proportions deviate likely due to COVID-19 affecting the methods of detection for measles cases. Lab confirmed method starts to become more of a feasible method. The clinical versus lab confirmed push and pull against each other with the epi-linked method remaining around the same percentage for the years.

Association between a country’s air quality and number of measles cases

There are many factors that can contribute to why measles cases fluctuate and one of those factos is air quality. We have data about number of measles cases in each country and we are interested if there is a link between air quality of that country and their number of measles cases for the year 2025. To answer this question, we first need to examine the relationship between a country’s Air Quality Index (AQI) and its measles burden. To do this, we average each country’s suspected measles cases across all available years and applied a log10 transformation to account for the highly skewed distribution of case counts. Plotting the log10 transformed average annual cases by AQI allows us to visually assess whether any linear trend exists between air pollution levels and measles burden before formalizing the relationship through a regression model.

Code 
meta_df |>
  select(iso3, country, year, gdp_per_capita_nominal, 
         measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population,
         measles_lab_confirmed, measles_total) |>
  glimpse()
Rows: 2,396
Columns: 7
$ iso3                                                <chr> "DZA", "DZA", "DZA…
$ country                                             <chr> "Algeria", "Algeri…
$ year                                                <dbl> 2012, 2013, 2014, …
$ gdp_per_capita_nominal                              <dbl> 6057.974, 5997.896…
$ measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population <dbl> 1.46, 0.00, 0.00, …
$ measles_lab_confirmed                               <dbl> 2, 0, 0, 2, 21, 74…
$ measles_total                                       <dbl> 55, 0, 0, 62, 49, …

Table 3: AQI vs. Average Measles Cases

Code 
summary_vars <- c("total_aqi", "measles_total", "measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population")

filtered_df <- meta_df %>%
  filter(!is.na(total_aqi), !is.na(measles_total), 
         !is.na(measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population))

country_summary <- summary_vars %>%
  map(~ filtered_df %>%
        group_by(country) %>%
        summarise(!!.x := mean(.data[[.x]], na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  ) %>%
  reduce(left_join, by = "country") %>%
  mutate(aqi_category = case_when(
    total_aqi <= 50  ~ "Good",
    total_aqi <= 100 ~ "Moderate",
    total_aqi <= 150 ~ "Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups",
    total_aqi >  150 ~ "Unhealthy"
  )) %>%
  arrange(desc(total_aqi))

country_summary %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_label(
    country      = "Country",
    total_aqi    = "Mean AQI",
    aqi_category = "AQI Category",
    measles_total = "Mean Annual Cases",
    measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population = "Incidence Rate (per 1,000,000)"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = total_aqi, decimals = 1) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = measles_total, decimals = 1) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population, decimals = 2) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label   = "Air Quality",
    columns = c(total_aqi, aqi_category)
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label   = "Measles Burden",
    columns = c(measles_total, measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population)
  ) %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Air Quality and Measles Burden by Country",
    subtitle = "166 countries ordered by mean AQI (highest to lowest)"
  ) %>%
  data_color(
    columns = total_aqi,
    palette = c("lightgreen", "lightblue", "dodgerblue", "blue"),
    domain  = c(0, 200)
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_spanners()
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    footnote  = "Mean values calculated across all available years per country.",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = c(measles_total, 
                                                measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population))
  )

Burundi 8.0 Good 506.9 39.65
Madagascar 8.0 Good 16,139.1 572.83
South Sudan 8.0 Good 889.7 80.88
Kiribati 7.0 Good 0.0 0.00
Micronesia (Federated States of) 6.0 Good 29.1 268.86
Tuvalu 6.0 Good 0.0 0.00
United Republic of Tanzania 6.0 Good 557.7 9.77
Nauru 5.0 Good 0.0 0.00
Marshall Islands 4.0 Good 0.0 0.00
Central African Republic 3.0 Good 610.6 123.73
1 Mean values calculated across all available years per country.

The mean annual cases for countries with very high AQI are not all super high or super low and instead are both. The US has an unhealthy air quality with almost 400 cases but for the Democratic People’s republic of Korea who also has unhealthy air quality, they have a mean number of measles case of 0.6. These two cases might be outliers or anomalies and a link between air quality and measles cases could still be linked if we broaden our spectrum and look not jsut at individual countries. To see if there is an association with AQI and measles cases we can fit a model to these data.

Figure 3: Linear Regression between Air Quality Index and log10 transformed Average Measles Cases by Country

Code 
meta_df %>%
  filter(!is.na(total_aqi), !is.na(total_suspected_measles_rubella_cases)) %>%
  group_by(country, total_aqi) %>%
  summarise(avg_cases = mean(total_suspected_measles_rubella_cases, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = total_aqi, y = avg_cases)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "dodgerblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "red") +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Air Quality Index vs. Average Measles Cases by Country",
    subtitle = "Each point represents one country; cases shown on log scale",
    x = "Air Quality Index (higher = worse air quality)",
    y = "Average Annual Measles Cases (log scale)",
    caption = "Sources: API Ninjas AQI data; TidyTuesday measles dataset"
  )

The scatterplot shows a slight negative linear association between AQI and average measles cases. Observing the spread of the points,countries with low AQI show a wider vertical spread of average measles cases than countries with higher quality index. The fanning pattern could indicate that there is heteroscedasticity and could a formal test could be done to determine if there is an association.

Model

Table 4: Linear Regression Coefficients

Code 
aqi_model <- meta_df |>
  filter(!is.na(total_aqi), !is.na(total_suspected_measles_rubella_cases)) |>
  group_by(country, total_aqi) |>
  summarise(avg_cases = mean(total_suspected_measles_rubella_cases, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(log_avg_cases = log10(avg_cases + 1))

model <- lm(log_avg_cases ~ total_aqi, data = aqi_model)

wide_model <- broom::tidy(model, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    term = recode(term,
      "(Intercept)" = "Intercept",
      "total_aqi" = "Air Quality Index"
    ), conf_int = str_c("(", round(conf.low, 3), ", ", round(conf.high, 3), ")")
  ) %>%
  select(term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, conf_int) %>%
  rename(c(Term = term, Estimate = estimate, SE = std.error, Statistic = statistic,
           "P-value" = p.value, "Conf. Interval" = conf_int))

wide_model |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = -"Conf. Interval", decimals = 3) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
                              locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_body(
              columns = Term,
              rows = Term %in% c("Intercept", "Air Quality Index")
            )) |>
  tab_caption(caption = "Table 4. Linear Regression coefficients for the association between measles cases and AQI.")
A table showing the linear regression coefficients for the association between measles cases and AQI
Term Estimate SE Statistic P-value Conf. Interval
Intercept 2.448 0.130 18.864 0.000 (2.192, 2.704)
Air Quality Index 0.001 0.001 0.603 0.547 (-0.002, 0.004)

Based on the test statistic of 0.603 and large p-value (0.547 > 0.05), we have extremely weak evidence against the null hypothesis. We can conclude that there is no association between average measles cases and AQI. Also, because 0 is contained in the 95% confidence interval (-0.002 to 0.004), we cannot say that the true effect of AQI on measles cases is 0. There is no significant linear association between AQI and average measles cases after a log transformation. Future analysis could include an additional predictor such as GDP per capita to observe if there is a more meaningful conclusion that could have been drawn.

Table 5: Measles Burden by AQI Quartile

Code 
summarise_by_quartile <- function(df, var, n_tiles = 4) {
  
  quartile_labels <- c("Q1 (Lowest AQI)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (Highest AQI)")
  
  df %>%
    filter(!is.na({{ var }}), !is.na(measles_total), !is.na(measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population)) %>%
    mutate(
      quartile = ntile({{ var }}, n_tiles),
      quartile = factor(quartile, labels = quartile_labels)
    ) %>%
    group_by(quartile) %>%
    summarise(
      n_countries    = n_distinct(country),
      mean_aqi       = mean({{ var }},       na.rm = TRUE),
      total_cases    = sum(measles_total,    na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_incidence = mean(measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population,       na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
}

# call the function
quartile_summary <- summarise_by_quartile(meta_df, total_aqi)

quartile_summary %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_label(
    quartile       = "AQI Quartile",
    n_countries    = "No. Countries",
    mean_aqi       = "Mean AQI",
    total_cases    = "Total Cases",
    mean_incidence = "Mean Incidence Rate"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean_aqi, mean_incidence), decimals = 2) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = total_cases, decimals = 0) %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Measles Burden Across AQI Quartiles",
    subtitle = "Does measles burden increase with worsening air quality?"
  ) %>%
  data_color(columns = mean_aqi, palette = c("lightblue", "dodgerblue", "blue"))
A table showing the mean AQI for each AQI quartile and its corresponding total cases and mean incidence rate
Measles Burden Across AQI Quartiles
Does measles burden increase with worsening air quality?
AQI Quartile No. Countries Mean AQI Total Cases Mean Incidence Rate
Q1 (Lowest AQI) 39 13.03 959,010 62.06
Q2 45 39.69 574,436 35.11
Q3 44 79.14 1,173,118 24.94
Q4 (Highest AQI) 41 148.99 521,665 87.56

Countries with low AQI (Q1) and countries with high AQI (Q4) have around a median 500 of measles cases. This finding is consistent with the linear regression model having a weak association. Countries with low AQI have a bimodal distribution, indicating that they are not a homogeneous group. From this observation, there may be a divide between high-income and well-vaccinated countries and low income countries that also have low AQI, but a weaker healthcare system.

Figure 4: Measles Distribution by AQI Quartile

Code 
aqi_model |>
  mutate(
    aqi_quartile = ntile(total_aqi, 4),
    aqi_label = case_when(
      aqi_quartile == 1 ~ "Q1 · low AQI",
      aqi_quartile == 2 ~ "Q2",
      aqi_quartile == 3 ~ "Q3",
      aqi_quartile == 4 ~ "Q4 · high AQI"
    ) |> factor(levels = c("Q4 · high AQI","Q3","Q2","Q1 · low AQI"))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = log_avg_cases, y = aqi_label, fill = aqi_label)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 1.4,
    alpha = 0.8,
    quantile_lines = TRUE,
    quantiles = 2        # draws the median line
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
  breaks = log10(c(1, 10, 100, 500, 1000, 5000, 10000, 100000)),
  labels = c("1", "10", "100", "500", "1k", "5k", "10k", "100k")
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#CECBF6","#FAC775","#9FE1CB","#B5D4F4")) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of measles cases by AQI quartile",
    x = "Avg annual measles cases (log scale)",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_ridges() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Our guess that AQI plays a role in the rise and fall of measles cases was wrong as there seems to be no significant difference between countries of different AQIs and their mean measles cases.
